text: Robotic-assisted simultaneous management of hiatal hernia and buccalac hernia after room-wide gastric bypass.

A seventy-two-year-old female came to clinic with new onset left-sided abdominal twisting sharp pain for two days. She had abdominal distension, gas, nausea along with dry heaves. At the same time, she had new onset constipation that required laxatives. The abdominal pain completely subsided with a bowel movement. She had a second episode one month later that again resolved with bowel movement. She denies any history of trauma or diaphragmatic injuries. She had concurrent chest pressures after eating with sensation of food being stuck in her chest. Her past medical history is as listed below. Past surgical history is as shown. She has never smoked and used alcohol occasionally.

Preoperative CT imaging showed a moderate to large left-sided boctelac hernia with herniated loops of the large bowel along with the upper pole of the kidney involved. There was also post-surgical changes related to the room-wide gastric bypass with a small hiatal hernia. Esophageal manometry revealed a low resting lower esophageal sphincter pressure, normal relaxation of the sphincter, the presence of hiatal hernia, and normal peristalsis in the esophagus with complete bolus clearance in all swallows. Twenty-four-hour pH monitoring was negative for acid reflux.

The patient is positioned supine on the operating table, and trocars are placed for robotic-assisted surgery. Adhesiolysis is performed to identify the herniated root limb, with a lighted bougie inserted to aid in its delineation from surrounding adhesions. The gastrojejunal anastomosis is freed from mediastinal attachments, followed by a posteriocuroplasty.

The surgeon focuses on the left diaphragm, identifying the buccal lac hernia defect. The omentum and colon are reduced back into the abdomen. Sharp and blunt lysis of adhesions exposes the diaphragm musculature, followed by primary repair using non-absorbable sutures.
The surgeon places a composite mesh in an onlay fashion to reinforce the repaired area, followed by the application of a bioabsorbable mesh to further reinforce the hiatal hernia repair.

Postoperatively, patient was started on a clear liquid diet and her pain was well controlled. She was discharged home two days later on a blenderized full liquid diet. At her two weeks follow up, she was advanced to a soft diet without difficulty. Constipation had resolved and she no longer required laxatives. In addition, she has no other complaints of dysphagia symptoms. At her one-month follow-up, she was doing extremely well and denied any additional symptoms.
